paper1.question:
Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word.

The principal gave the students a distinguished award for their community service.
paper1.explanation: 'Distinguished' means eminent or outstanding. Its opposite is 'ordinary' — unremarkable or undistinguished.

paper1.question:
Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word.

She was known throughout the village for her compassionate response to those in need.
paper1.explanation: 'Compassionate' means showing sympathy and care. Its opposite is 'cruel' — deliberately causing pain or suffering.

paper1.question:
Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word.

The lattice windows were the most conspicuous feature of the old colonial building.
paper1.explanation: 'Conspicuous' means easily visible or attracting attention. Its opposite is 'insignificant' — not noticeable or important.

paper1.question:
Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word.

It seemed probable that the school would introduce the new policy next term.
paper1.explanation: 'Probable' means likely to happen. Its opposite is 'unlikely' — not expected or probable.

paper1.question:
Choose the word closest to OPPOSITE in meaning to the underlined word.

Many teenagers do not agree with the amount of restrictiveness in today's schools.
paper1.explanation: 'Restrictiveness' means imposing many rules and limits. Its opposite is 'permissiveness' — allowing greater freedom with fewer rules.

paper1.question:
One of the underlined words may be misspelt. Choose the letter (A), (B) or (C) of the misspelt word. If no word is misspelt, choose (D).

It is my (A) pleasure to give you some information concerning proper (B) nutrition and (C) excersize.
paper1.explanation: 'Excersize' is misspelt. The correct spelling is 'exercise'.

paper1.question:
One of the underlined words may be misspelt. Choose the letter (A), (B) or (C) of the misspelt word. If no word is misspelt, choose (D).

The nurses were given (A) seperate quarters because the main building did not have (B) sufficient room for their (C) accommodation.
paper1.explanation: 'Seperate' is misspelt. The correct spelling is 'separate'.

paper1.question:
One of the underlined words may be misspelt. Choose the letter (A), (B) or (C) of the misspelt word. If no word is misspelt, choose (D).

The (A) irate parent's arguments merely helped to further (B) aggravate the (C) untollerable situation.
paper1.explanation: 'Untollerable' is misspelt. The correct spelling is 'intolerable'.

paper1.question:
One of the underlined words may be misspelt. Choose the letter (A), (B) or (C) of the misspelt word. If no word is misspelt, choose (D).

His (A) intelligence rather than his (B) hieght was the (C) decisive factor in his promotion.
paper1.explanation: 'Hieght' is misspelt. The correct spelling is 'height'. Remember: 'i before e except after c'.

paper1.question:
One of the underlined words may be misspelt. Choose the letter (A), (B) or (C) of the misspelt word. If no word is misspelt, choose (D).

The committee was (A) unanimous in its (B) recommendation that the project should (C) proceed immediately.
paper1.explanation: All three words are correctly spelt. The answer is (D) No error.

paper1.question:
The sentence below is followed by four sentences (A–D). Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original.

Much is being done to develop tourism by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.
options: It is important to provide good hotel accommodation and facilities for tourists.
Good hotel accommodation and facilities are crucial if we want to develop tourism.
We are doing a great deal to boost tourism by providing good hotel accommodation and facilities.
If we do not provide good hotel accommodation and facilities, tourists will not come.
paper1.explanation: Option C preserves the active present continuous meaning ('is being done' / 'are doing') and the same cause-effect relationship as the original sentence.

paper1.question:
Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original.

John told his parents in no uncertain terms that he was going to university as soon as he left school.
options: John boasted to his parents that he was going to university as soon as he left school.
John told his parents that they should permit him to go to university after he left school.
John made it quite clear to his parents that when he left school he would go to university immediately.
John's parents were told that it was obviously his desire to attend university immediately after leaving school.
paper1.explanation: 'In no uncertain terms' means clearly and directly. Option C captures this — 'made it quite clear' — without adding the suggestion of boasting (A) or requesting permission (B).

paper1.question:
Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original.

So engrossed was he in his task that he did not hear his sister enter the room.
options: His task was so difficult that he did not observe his sister's entry.
He did not hear his sister enter the room because of the job he was doing.
His sister's entry did not attract his attention because he was enjoying what he was doing.
He was so preoccupied with what he was doing that he was unaware of his sister's entry.
paper1.explanation: 'Engrossed' means deeply absorbed. Option D uses 'preoccupied' and 'unaware' to preserve the core meaning — he was absorbed and did not notice his sister — without introducing new ideas like 'difficulty' (A) or 'enjoying' (C).

paper1.question:
Rewrite the sentence beginning with the words given. Choose the correct word or phrase to complete it.

Original: 'The cricket match ended abruptly when many of the spectators stormed the field.'
Begin: 'Many of the spectators stormed the field...'
options: so the cricket match ended abruptly.
but the cricket match ended abruptly.
getting the cricket match to end abruptly.
preventing the cricket match from ending abruptly.
paper1.explanation: 'Many of the spectators stormed the field SO the cricket match ended abruptly.' The word 'so' shows that the storming of the field caused the abrupt ending, preserving the original cause-and-effect meaning.

paper1.question:
Choose the sentence NEAREST in meaning to the original.

I cannot understand why you have done this since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you.
options: Since Mark means a lot to you, the reason for your action is puzzling to me.
Since you tell me that Mark means a lot to you, he cannot understand why you have done this.
You tell me that although Mark means a lot to you, you did this.
Mark cannot mean a lot to you or else I know you would not have done this.
paper1.explanation: Option A preserves all the key elements: Mark's importance, the speaker's confusion, and the contradiction between the stated feeling and the action taken.

paper1.question:
Some sentences below are unacceptable because of inappropriate grammar, idiom or vocabulary. Select the ONE underlined part (A), (B) or (C) that is inappropriate. If the sentence is acceptable, choose (D).

'Mary, will you please (A) full up the (B) buckets before the (C) water supply is cut off?'
paper1.explanation: 'Full up' is non-standard. The correct idiomatic phrase is 'fill up'. The rest of the sentence is grammatically correct.

paper1.question:
Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate. If acceptable, choose (D).

'(A) Peter have dengue and (B) feels weak, so he is (C) unable to participate in the games.'
paper1.explanation: 'Peter have' is incorrect. Peter is a singular subject and requires the singular verb 'has'. The correct form is 'Peter has dengue'.

paper1.question:
Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate. If acceptable, choose (D).

'(A) Too much students (B) entered the competition; (C) only one of them was good enough to reach the finals.'
paper1.explanation: 'Too much students' is incorrect. 'Students' is a countable noun and requires 'too many'. The correct phrase is 'Too many students'.

paper1.question:
Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate. If acceptable, choose (D).

'Overcome by fear, I (A) ran passed my home (B) without realizing that (C) I had done so.'
paper1.explanation: 'Ran passed' is incorrect. 'Passed' is a verb (past tense of 'pass') but here 'past' is needed as a preposition or adverb meaning beyond. The correct phrase is 'ran past my home'.

paper1.question:
Select the ONE underlined part that is inappropriate. If acceptable, choose (D).

'The (A) better of the two athletes (B) claim never (C) to have used the dangerous drugs found in his possession.'
paper1.explanation: When comparing exactly two people, 'better' is correct (not 'best'). However, 'the better of the two athletes' is singular and requires the singular verb 'claims', not 'claim'.

paper1.question:
Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word.

It was impossible to rectify the error made by the computer.
paper1.explanation: 'Rectify' means to put right or correct a mistake. 'Correct' is the nearest synonym.

paper1.question:
Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word.

The flowers grew profusely in the garden after the heavy rains.
paper1.explanation: 'Profusely' means in large amounts or plentifully. 'Abundantly' is the nearest synonym.

paper1.question:
Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word.

The news story revealed the squalor of the abandoned building.
paper1.explanation: 'Squalor' means dirty and unpleasant conditions resulting from poverty or neglect. 'Filthiness' is the nearest synonym.

paper1.question:
Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word.

During the rainstorm yesterday, the wind blew incessantly across the island.
paper1.explanation: 'Incessantly' means without stopping or pausing; continually. 'Constantly' is the nearest synonym.

paper1.question:
Choose the word NEAREST in meaning to the underlined word.

For the first time in a decade, the company disclosed a deficit in its account.
paper1.explanation: A 'deficit' is an excess of expenditure over income — a shortfall or financial gap. 'Shortfall' is the nearest synonym.

paper1.passage:
BEAT THAT LIGHT

I think the horses must be laughing,
Kicking up their heels and chaffing,
Watching from their green abode
The things that drove them off the road,
5  Whinnying in soft derision
At breakdown, blowout and collision,
Neighing, as they roam the prairies,
Motorists' obituaries.

Read the epitaphs on Monday
10  Of drivers various and Sunday;
Beep the horn and howl the klaxon
For Hebrew, Latin, and Anglo-Saxon;
Howling klaxon, beeping horn,
The funeral dirge on Monday morn,
15  Usher out the unlucky drivers
Without convincing the survivors
Here's a curve and here's a truck,
Take a chance, and trust to luck,
The next one's practically standing still.
paper1.question: Why does the poet suggest the horses are laughing (line 1)?
options: He can hear them laughing in the fields.
The prairie is now entirely theirs to roam.
They are treated well by their new owners.
They were replaced by something that causes harm and death.
paper1.explanation: The horses were 'drove off the road' by motor vehicles. Now they watch from their green abode as these vehicles suffer breakdowns, accidents and deaths — the very things that replaced them are destroying their replacements.

paper1.question: What are 'the things' suggested in line 4?
paper1.explanation: 'The things that drove them off the road' refers to motor vehicles (cars, trucks) which replaced horses as the primary means of transport.

paper1.question: Which of the following words BEST suggests the drivers' destiny?
paper1.explanation: 'Obituaries' — notices of death — most directly suggest the fatal destiny of the reckless drivers. The poem implies they are likely to die.

paper1.question: According to the poet, what days seemed most prone to fatalities?
paper1.explanation: Lines 9-10 read 'the epitaphs on Monday / Of drivers various and Sunday' — the epitaphs (death notices) are written on Monday about those who died on Sunday, suggesting Sundays are the most fatal days.

paper1.question: What is the poet's tone in lines 17-18 ('Here's a curve and here's a truck, / Take a chance, and trust to luck')?
paper1.explanation: The poet sarcastically mimics the reckless driver's carefree attitude — 'take a chance, trust to luck' — mocking those who gamble with their lives. The tone is darkly ironic and sarcastic.

paper1.question: Through this poem, the poet is ultimately recommending that drivers drive:
paper1.explanation: By satirising reckless driving and showing its fatal consequences (obituaries, funeral dirges, epitaphs), the poet implicitly advocates for careful, responsible driving.

paper1.question: Which of the following means NEARLY the same as the title 'Beat That Light'?
options: Run through a traffic light before it turns red
paper1.explanation: 'Beating a light' is an idiom meaning to drive through a traffic light just as or just after it turns red — the poem is about reckless driving, and 'Beat That Light' captures this dangerous behaviour.

paper1.passage:
She had always been a small woman — short, light-boned, delicately structured — but now, under the white hospital sheet, she was becoming tiny. I thought of a doll with huge, fierce eyes. There had always been a fierceness in her. It showed in that angry, challenging thrust of the chin when she issued an opinion, and a great one she had always been for issuing opinions.

'I tell people exactly what's on my mind,' she had been fond of boasting. 'I tell them what I think, whether they like it or not.' Often they had not liked it. She could be sarcastic to people in whom she detected evidence of the ignoramus or the fool.

And so she was. A formidable woman. Determined to speak her mind, determined to bend those who opposed her. In that time when I had known her best, my mother had hurled herself at life with chin thrust forward, eyes blazing, and an energy that made her seem always on the run.

She ran after squawking chickens, a knife in her hand. She ran when she made the beds, ran when she set the table. Life was combat, and victory was not for the lazy, the timid, the slugabed, the mushmouth afraid to tell people exactly what was on their mind. She ran.

But now her running was over.

— Adapted from Russell Baker, In My Day
paper1.question: The mother's 'thrust of the chin' (paragraph 1) is done in:
paper1.explanation: The chin thrust accompanies the issuing of challenging opinions — it is a gesture of defiance and challenge, indicating she is asserting herself boldly.

paper1.passage:

paper1.question: The writer says she could be 'sarcastic to people in whom she detected evidence of the ignoramus or the fool.' This tells us she had little patience for people who were:
paper1.explanation: An 'ignoramus' is an ignorant person and a 'fool' is a stupid person — she was sarcastic toward those she considered unintelligent or foolish.

paper1.question: Why does the writer choose the expression 'A formidable woman' to describe the mother?
options: He is intimidated by her directness.
Her words often provoked anger in others.
It emphasises the contrast between her past strength and her present frailty.
He wants to emphasise her strength and energy, which he finds admirable.
paper1.explanation: 'Formidable' here is used admiringly — the writer gives examples of her energy, determination and refusal to be meek. The word choice reflects his admiration for her forceful character.

paper1.question: What literary device is used in 'my mother had hurled herself at life'?
paper1.explanation: 'Hurled herself at life' is a metaphor — it compares her approach to life to physically throwing oneself into something, emphasising her fierce energy and determination. It also creates a vivid image.

paper1.question: The repetition of 'She ran' throughout paragraph 4 is used primarily to:
options: show that the mother was always in a hurry and disorganised
emphasise the relentless energy and pace with which she lived her life
suggest she was being chased by something dangerous
imply she was always running away from her problems
paper1.explanation: The repeated 'She ran' creates a rhythmic, insistent effect that mirrors the mother's own tireless energy. Combined with 'Life was combat', it emphasises her unstoppable drive and vitality.

paper1.question: The final sentence, 'But now her running was over', implies that the mother has:
options: died or is close to death, leaving the son to reflect on her life
paper1.explanation: The hospital setting (opening paragraph), the description of her 'becoming tiny', and the finality of 'But now her running was over' — after the extended metaphor of 'running' as living life fully — implies she has died or is dying.

paper1.question: The writer's overall tone toward his mother can best be described as:
paper1.explanation: Despite describing her sharp tongue and forceful manner, the writer's language shows deep admiration ('formidable woman', the energy he celebrates) and deep affection — he is clearly moved by her condition and life.

paper1.passage:
Industrialisation has created a new set of intangible threats to health. They have presented themselves in socially accepted forms: the socially acquired glamour of cigarette smoking, the pleasures of alcohol and the spurious release gained through drugs have resulted in many cases of heart disease, cancer and debility.

Since many developing countries are succumbing to the same habits and dependence, they lay themselves open to the same diseases. One can work by one rough rule-of-thumb: once a country achieves a life expectancy at birth of 60 years, cancer rapidly moves up in the ranking of health problems and causes of death.

Nothing illustrates these aspects more succinctly than smoking. About a quarter of all cigarette smokers are killed before their time by tobacco. Most would have lived on for another 5 to 30 years. If they stopped smoking before they developed cancer or heart or lung disease, they could have averted the risk of dying from tobacco.

Government policies can strengthen individual choice and prevent the growth of the smoking habit. Restrictions on advertising and promoting cigarettes will reduce incentives to smoke; raising the cost of cigarettes lowers consumption. Health education should promote a way of life free of smoking.

— Source Unknown
paper1.question: According to paragraph 1, deaths from heart disease, cancer and debility are on the increase because of:
options: the dangers posed by modern living generally
growing substance abuse in today's society
longer life expectancy in recent years
paper1.explanation: Paragraph 1 specifically names 'cigarette smoking, alcohol and drugs' as the causes — these are forms of substance abuse presented in socially accepted ways.

paper1.passage:
Industrialisation has created a new set of intangible threats to health. They have presented themselves in socially accepted forms: the socially acquired glamour of cigarette smoking, the pleasures of alcohol and the spurious release gained through drugs have resulted in many cases of heart disease, cancer and debility.

Since many developing countries are succumbing to the same habits and dependence, they lay themselves open to the same diseases. One can work by one rough rule-of-thumb: once a country achieves a life expectancy at birth of 60 years, cancer rapidly moves up in the ranking of health problems and causes of death.

Nothing illustrates these aspects more succinctly than smoking. About a quarter of all cigarette smokers are killed before their time by tobacco. Most would have lived on for another 5 to 30 years. If they stopped smoking before they developed cancer or heart or lung disease, they could have averted the risk of dying from tobacco.

Government policies can strengthen individual choice and prevent the growth of the smoking habit. Restrictions on advertising and promoting cigarettes will reduce incentives to smoke; raising the cost of cigarettes lowers consumption. Health education should promote a way of life free of smoking.

— Source Unknown
paper1.question: The words 'succumbing' and 'dependence' (paragraph 2) imply that the habits of smoking, alcohol and drug use:
options: are created by the users themselves
give rise to a state of numbness
have an overpowering effect on people
paper1.explanation: 'Succumbing' means yielding to something difficult to resist, and 'dependence' implies addiction — both suggest these habits overpower people's willpower and control.

paper1.passage:

Government policies can strengthen individual choice and prevent the growth of the smoking habit. Restrictions on advertising and promoting cigarettes will reduce incentives to smoke; raising the cost of cigarettes lowers consumption. Health education should promote a way of life free of smoking.

— Source Unknown
paper1.question: According to paragraph 3, smokers who give up before developing disease would:
options: reduce but not eliminate their health risk
paper1.explanation: The passage states they 'could have averted the risk of dying from tobacco' — they would avoid death from tobacco, though the passage does not guarantee complete avoidance of all health problems.

paper1.question: One reason people smoke, as suggested in the passage, is because cigarette smoking:
options: is fashionable and glamorous in modern society
provides release and relaxation for individuals
paper1.explanation: Paragraph 1 refers to 'the socially acquired glamour of cigarette smoking' — suggesting people smoke because of its perceived social glamour and fashionableness.

paper1.question: The writer's overall purpose in this passage is to:
options: entertain readers with facts about smoking
inform readers about health threats from modern habits and argue for preventive action
criticise developing countries for poor health policies
paper1.explanation: The passage combines factual information about health risks with a call for government and individual action — informing AND persuading. It is neither pure entertainment nor a simple ban argument.

paper1.question: Which of the following is NOT suggested in the passage as a way to reduce smoking?
paper1.explanation: The passage suggests advertising restrictions, price increases and health education — prison sentences are never mentioned as a strategy.

paper1.passage:
HE'S THE MAN

Ladies and Gentlemen, I present to you the candidate for Dimsville. He is the man who will make all your dreams come true. He is the man who will fulfil all your wishes. He is the man who will stand by you through thick and thin. Our opponents say that he comes from a foreign country and he is not one of you. I must thank them for their kind comments because that is just what you need — his exposure to a developed world. He is equipped with the best ideas that will create opportunities for you.

Don't you need steady jobs? And how about a school like that which the other areas have? Yes, my friends. You need to live like valued citizens. Not like oppressed slaves in endless destitution.

Your lives are not a journey into the Promised Land. You wander in a vast wilderness where hostile forces of humanity hound you with relentless cruelty. Your daily bread is sickness and worry, hunger and disease. Your hovels are dens of misery filled with empty hopes and despair. Have you considered the future of your children?

Look at this man; the man who will make your dreams come true; the man who will give you your wishes; the man who cares enough to come to your village.

— Source Unknown
paper1.question: The purpose of this speech is to:
options: introduce a new product to the community
find out the needs of the audience
win votes for a political candidate
condemn the efforts of the opponents
paper1.explanation: The speech introduces a political candidate, highlights the community's problems, attacks opponents and asks the audience to support this candidate — it is a political campaign speech.

— Source Unknown
paper1.question: The speech was MOST likely given at a:
paper1.explanation: The speech introduces a 'candidate', addresses voters as 'my friends', criticises opponents, and appeals to community needs — all hallmarks of a political meeting/rally.

paper1.question: The speaker addresses the audience as 'you' in order to:
options: accuse the audience of failing their duty
make the audience feel he understands and cares about them
paper1.explanation: Direct address using 'you' creates intimacy and connection. By repeatedly saying 'you need', 'your lives', 'your children', the speaker makes the audience feel personally seen and cared for.

paper1.question: The speaker suggests that because the candidate 'comes from a foreign country' he will:
options: leave the community at any time
be an advantage to the community
paper1.explanation: The speaker cleverly turns the opponents' attack into a positive — foreign experience means 'exposure to a developed world' and 'the best ideas', making it an advantage rather than a drawback.

paper1.question: In paragraph 1, the speaker repeats 'He is the man who...' primarily to:
options: introduce the candidate's name to the audience
impress on the audience that this is the best person for the job
inform the audience that they need a man rather than a woman
paper1.explanation: Anaphora — the repeated 'He is the man who...' — builds emphasis and rhythm, hammering home the message that this particular candidate is the solution to all the community's problems.

paper1.question: The name 'Dimsville' is suitable for this village because:
options: it is named after a famous person
there is no electricity in the village
the villagers' hopes are dim due to poverty and neglect
paper1.explanation: 'Dim' means without light or brightness and by extension hopeless and bleak. The name 'Dimsville' reflects the community's desperate conditions — dim prospects, poverty, sickness and despair.

paper1.question: Which of the following persuasive devices is NOT used in the speech?
paper1.explanation: The speech uses rhetorical questions ('Don't you need steady jobs?'), repetition ('He is the man who...') and emotive/exaggerated language ('oppressed slaves', 'dens of misery') — but presents no statistics or factual data.

paper1.question: 'Your daily bread is sickness and worry, hunger and disease' is an example of:
options: Simile — comparing their lives to bread
Metaphor — describing their daily existence as being made of suffering
Alliteration — the repetition of consonant sounds
paper1.explanation: This is a metaphor — 'daily bread' literally means food/sustenance but here their daily sustenance IS sickness and worry. It uses the familiar concept of bread (daily needs) to describe what their lives are actually filled with.

paper1.passage:
NATIONAL YOUTH SYMPOSIUM
TOPIC: ADOLESCENT HEALTH — THE CHALLENGES
OBANA CONFERENCE CENTRE
SUNDAY, 22 AUGUST

Sponsors: Ministry of Youth | Ministry of Education | National Council on Drug Abuse | Conference of Churches | University Christian Council | Muslim Youth Association | Hindu Youth Council

Who should attend: Interested members of the public | Members of youth groups | Health care professionals | Students in medical sciences | Media personnel

How to attend: Contact Yvonne Dalton at the Ministry of Youth. Tel: 111 2615. Complete the registration form and return to the Ministry of Youth.

SESSIONS: HIV/AIDS — Dr L. Bissanda | Teen Pregnancy — Prof. Nancy Sewell | Other STDs — Dr C. Smith-Browne | Alcohol and Other Legal Drugs — Major Leonard Akansa | Human Sexuality — Rev. Sementhia Been | Healthy Food — Dr Prakash Ramdat | Athletes and Performance-enhancing Drugs — Bragna Deetha | Prostitution — Cantona Bennett

Testimonials from: Young entrepreneur of the year | A recovering cocaine user | A contestant in the Miss World competition

Feature Address by 'Most Conscious Youth of the Year': Bukawela Tudor

Deadline for submission of forms: 16 July
paper1.question: According to the information provided, the MAIN theme of the symposium is:
paper1.explanation: The topic is clearly stated as 'ADOLESCENT HEALTH — THE CHALLENGES'. Drug abuse and STDs are sub-topics within this main theme.

paper1.question: Which word can BEST replace 'symposium' in the advertisement?
paper1.explanation: A symposium is a formal meeting at which experts discuss a particular subject. 'Conference' is the closest substitute — more formal than discussion or conversation, and more focused than an exhibition.
